input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a six‑axis serial robot (MABI MAX 100) used in a machining cell. Each axis consists of an electric motor, gearbox, encoder and joint‑level sensors (torque, temperature). The robot operates under a 1 kHz real‑time control loop, offers a repeatability of ±0.02 mm and a per‑axis load capacity of up to 10 kg, providing a modular, high‑precision platform that is well suited for digital‑twin representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides continuous multi‑physics‑aware identification of joint friction, real‑time health monitoring, predictive‑maintenance alerts, and an interactive web dashboard visualizing friction estimates, temperature trends and kinematic states. It also supports motion‑planning optimisation by feeding back updated friction models and enables adaptive closed‑loop control. Target users are process engineers and maintenance personnel.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methods and a suite of modeling languages and tools. SysML (including block diagrams and internal block diagrams) is used to capture requirements, functional, logical, and physical architectures. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc describe components, connectors, and compositions, while MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state‑machine extensions provide behavioral semantics. Simulation and detailed dynamic analysis are performed with MathWorks Simulink. Generated code or executable models may be realized in general‑purpose programming languages such as C++ or Java. The approach integrates heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and supports multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functions, logic, physics) as described in the RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT methodologies.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is built upon the Digital Shadow Reference Model, which is presented as a UML class diagram defining key concepts such as DataTrace, ProcessingComponent, and Metadata together with their associations. A concrete Digital Twin is obtained by a conformance mapping that incarnates the reference classes into specific elements (e.g., concrete processing components and model artifacts). The ProcessingComponent encapsulates the computational logic (engineering, simulation, or data‑calculation models) and orchestrates data flow: it consumes input DataTraces, applies the associated models, and produces output DataTraces. Traceability is maintained via the ‘originates‑from’ association, allowing a transitive link from any output trace back to its generating component, input traces, and models. This class‑based composition constitutes the overall orchestration pattern, integrating heterogeneous MBSE artifacts (SysML, ADLs) with processing components to achieve systematic coordination and resource management within the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation is built on Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) artifacts. Structural and functional architectures are captured in SysML (including internal block diagrams) and may be translated into Architecture Description Languages such as ACME, AADL or MontiArc. Behavioral aspects are expressed with SysML state‑machine extensions or with MontiArc Automaton. These models are linked to executable simulation models in MathWorks Simulink and to source‑code implementations in C++ or Java. Data feeding the DT consist of contextual data traces that are aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose, as defined in the digital‑shadow reference model (UML class diagram).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The twin reflects a robot actuation chain comprising a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor that drives the first axis, coupled with gearboxes to transmit torque. Motion commands are issued by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840d sl controller, exposing remote control interfaces for supervisory commands and trajectory updates. Actuation range is limited by the motor’s torque and gearbox ratios; safety constraints are enforced by the controller’s built‑in monitoring functions. The controller’s addressing scheme follows the standard SINUMERIK protocol stack, allowing deterministic command delivery to the motor drive.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Each drive includes two position encoders: a motor‑mounted encoder directly on the motor shaft and a secondary encoder located after the gearbox. These sensors generate high‑resolution time‑series data that are streamed over MQTT to the Digital Twin’s data ingestion layer. While exact sampling frequencies are not stated, the architecture supports continuous high‑rate acquisition suitable for closed‑loop control, with data transmission rates governed by the MQTT broker’s QoS settings. Accuracy and precision are defined by the encoder specifications inherent to the robot’s design.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed through a traceability chain that links high‑level SysML specifications to lower‑level simulation models (e.g., Simulink) and to generated code. The reliability of the digital shadow depends on the correctness of the underlying physics‑based (white‑box) models and data‑driven (black‑box) models, as well as on the transformations applied to the data. The approach emphasizes verification of model transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models) and traceability of data provenance to ensure uncertainties are identified and managed.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin receives a one‑way automated data stream from the physical asset. Sensor measurements such as input current, output angle, torque and angular velocity are sampled at the device level, pre‑processed (e.g., filtered, unit‑converted) and published via the MQTT protocol to a broker that the Twin subscribes to. The publish‑subscribe topology enables real‑time ingestion; data are aggregated and abstracted according to the Digital Shadow reference model before being stored in the Twin’s contextual data repository. Event triggers can be periodic (fixed‑rate sampling) or condition‑based (threshold exceedance). Basic quality assurance (e.g., range checks) is applied during preprocessing, and the resulting trusted data are integrated into the Twin’s logical model for further analysis.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Doc 1 states that a Digital Twin can provide the active components required to push data back to the physical system, unlike a digital shadow which is passive. However, the provided documents do not describe concrete command types, message formats, authentication, validation, or confirmation mechanisms used for control flows from the digital twin to the physical asset.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between the physical system and the Digital Twin is realized through MQTT, a lightweight publish‑subscribe protocol that runs over TCP/IP. Sensors act as MQTT clients publishing measurement topics to an MQTT broker; the Twin acts as a subscriber client. This client‑server topology supports low‑latency, real‑time data delivery suitable for industrial sensor update rates. The network requirements are driven by the sampling frequencies of the physical sensors (typically tens of hertz), but specific bandwidth or latency figures are not detailed in the document. Reliability is achieved via MQTT's QoS levels, while security aspects (e.g., TLS) are not explicitly described.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The DT lifecycle follows the MBSE abstraction hierarchy: (1) requirements stage, (2) functional architecture stage, (3) logical architecture stage, and (4) product/technical (physical) stage. At each stage the DT is represented by model artifacts appropriate to the abstraction: SysML requirement diagrams, SysML internal block diagrams for functional and logical architecture, and concrete engineering models (e.g., Simulink, C++ code) for the product layer. Transitions between stages are managed by traceability links defined in the Digital Shadow reference model (the “originates‑from” association) which record which processing component produced each data trace, thus enabling controlled migration of model artefacts. Governance is achieved through the conformance relation of concrete realizations to the reference class diagram, ensuring that every instantiated model adheres to the defined domain concepts.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of the DT uses a model‑based systems‑engineering (MBSE) workflow that combines SysML with architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. Development methodologies cited include RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT, providing a structured sequence from requirements capture to physics‑based modeling. Quality assurance is supported by the traceability mechanism of the Digital Shadow reference model: each data trace records its originating processing component, input traces, and the simulation or data‑calculation model, enabling impact analysis and regression testing. Version control is implied by the need to maintain consistent mappings between concrete elements and reference concepts, and DT evolution is handled through successive concretizations of the reference model while preserving transitive “originates‑from” links.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides active processing components that ingest incoming data traces from the physical asset, apply simulation or data‑calculation models, and generate higher‑level output data that constitute the digital shadow. Additional metadata attached to the traces enables further data processing specific to digital‑twin scenarios. While the documents describe the computational pipeline (consumption of input traces, execution of processing logic, production of output data) and fine‑grained traceability, they do not specify concrete decision‑support functions, analytics algorithms, or user‑interaction mechanisms; therefore, the decision‑making capabilities are not detailed in the provided material.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The DT follows a reference‑model‑based standardization approach. A reference class diagram defines domain concepts and relationships, and a formal conformance relation (expressed as a mapping between concrete elements and reference concepts) guarantees that any concrete DT model conforms to the reference model. This provides an interoperability framework comparable to industry standards for model interchange, and the use of SysML and widely‑adopted ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) aligns the DT with established engineering standards.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction contains several specific inaccuracies and unsupported claims compared to the source text. The source states the robot has a path repeatability of '0.06 mm', not '±0.02 mm'. It does not mention a '1 kHz real-time control loop' or a 'per-axis load capacity of up to 10 kg'. While the text mentions 'live measurements' and 'encoders', it clarifies that 'The robot’s output torque is computed using a dynamics model' rather than directly measured by a torque sensor, and temperature is mentioned as a factor for friction models, not explicitly as a joint-level sensor in the hardware description.

The extraction correctly identifies the primary service as 'continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' from the abstract. However, it adds several unsupported details: the text does not specify an 'interactive web dashboard', 'predictive-maintenance alerts', 'motion-planning optimisation', or specific user roles like 'process engineers and maintenance personnel'. It focuses on improving 'robot motion accuracy' rather than the broader optimization and maintenance services claimed.

The extraction accurately identifies the core modeling enablers and methodologies discussed in the text, specifically Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), SysML, and MathWorks Simulink. It correctly captures the roles of these tools (e.g., SysML for structure, Simulink for simulation). However, it relies heavily on the background section to list potential tools (like ACME, AADL) rather than the specific implementation stack, and misses specific operational tools like MQTT and the Siemens controller. Evidence: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink.'

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions that the digital shadow involves 'live data', 'real-time' identification, and data that is 'streamed via MQTT'. However, it does not provide specific details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate regarding the presence of these specific technical details.

The document focuses on creating 'FrictionShadow' which acts as the identification element for 'one digital shadow' corresponding to the first axis of a single robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between different twins. Thus, 'Not in Document' is correct.

The description accurately captures the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM), its UML representation, the concretization process via conformance mapping, and the role of the ProcessingComponent in data flow and traceability. However, the extraction includes the phrase 'resource management' which is not supported by the source text, slightly reducing the score from perfect.

The document describes data flow from sensors 'streamed via MQTT' and the use of 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)', but this represents vertical integration (physical to digital). It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards for cross-system communication, or integration with other Digital Twins. The extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

block_3_judge.reasoning:
The extraction is highly accurate and detailed, perfectly matching the background and methodology sections of the source document. It correctly identifies the use of MBSE, SysML, ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), and the linkage to executable tools like Simulink and C++/Java. It also accurately defines the data component using the specific definition of a digital shadow found in the text.

Supported by quotes: "Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML", "ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs", "combined with further... tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java", "a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose".

The extraction correctly identifies the motor type (permanent-magnet synchronous), controller (Siemens SINUMERIK 840d sl), and the presence of gearboxes for the first axis. However, it includes unsupported technical details such as 'remote control interfaces for supervisory commands', 'controller’s addressing scheme follows the standard SINUMERIK protocol stack', and claims the actuation range is limited by 'motor’s torque and gearbox ratios' (the text attributes range limits to the safety cell). Supported by quotes: 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction accurately describes the presence of two encoders (motor-mounted and secondary) and the use of MQTT for streaming time-series data. However, it adds unsupported claims regarding 'high-resolution', 'closed-loop control' suitability, and 'MQTT broker’s QoS settings' governing transmission rates. It also speculates on accuracy being defined by encoder specifications, whereas the text only gives general robot accuracy figures. Supported by quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox', 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'.

The extraction provides a strong synthesis of the methodology regarding model traceability and types. It correctly identifies the link between SysML and simulation models (Simulink) and the reliance on white-box/black-box models for accuracy. The mention of traceability chains is well-supported by the text's description of the digital shadow reference model. While the specific terms 'verification of model transformations' and 'uncertainties... managed' are slightly interpretive, they are consistent with the text's emphasis on traceability for understanding data provenance and model behavior.

Supported by quotes: "trace a data trace back to its generating processing component, the consumed input traces, and the models involved", "using both white-box and black-box models to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy", "To describe behavior in more detail, these models are often combined with further... tools such as MathWorks Simulink".

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the specific data types (input current, output angle, torque) and the communication protocol (MQTT) mentioned in the text. It also accurately describes the one-way nature of the flow associated with a digital shadow. However, it adds unsupported technical details such as 'pre-processed (e.g., filtered, unit-converted)', 'periodic (fixed-rate sampling)', 'condition-based (threshold exceedance)', and 'range checks', which are not found in the source documents.

Accurately reflects the source text's distinction between digital shadows and digital twins, noting that a shadow is passive ('neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back') while a twin provides active components. It correctly identifies that the provided documents do not contain the technical implementation details (command types, formats, etc.) required by the characteristic definition.

While the extraction correctly identifies MQTT as the protocol used, it fabricates numerous technical details not present in the text, including 'TCP/IP', 'client-server topology', 'broker', 'subscriber', 'QoS levels', and 'TLS'. The document only mentions that data is 'streamed via MQTT' and references 'live-data linking', without specifying the underlying network architecture or specific sampling frequencies.

The extraction correctly states that the hosting and deployment infrastructure is 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on modeling, the robot's physical description, and data flows via MQTT but does not mention cloud/edge hosting, computational requirements, or deployment models.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the MBSE abstraction hierarchy and the specific model artifacts mentioned in the source. It correctly identifies the stages (requirements, functional, logical, product), the diagram types (SysML), and the transition/governance mechanisms (originates-from links, conformance relation). Quotes: 'modeling is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer', 'By following the originates from association, one can trace a data trace back to its generating processing component', 'Reference models describe concepts of a domain... and have “a conformance relation that precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the same language”'.

The description is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the text, correctly listing the methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), modeling languages (SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc), and the quality assurance mechanism via traceability. It also correctly links version control and evolution to the concretization of the reference model. Quotes: 'There exist different methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP... OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT', 'trace a data trace back to its generating processing component, the consumed input traces, and the models involved', 'concrete realization or concretization... incarnates the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations'.

Correctly identifies the processing components and the role of metadata while accurately noting the absence of specific high-level decision support features in the text. Supported by quotes: "A Processing component... consumes (uses) input data traces and produces output data", "Additional Metadata supports further data processing", and the text's focus on "understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss" rather than explicit decision-support algorithms.

The extraction perfectly summarizes the standardization approach described in Section II.B, focusing on the reference class diagram, the conformance relation, and the use of established engineering languages. It accurately reflects the text's description of how concrete models must conform to the reference concepts. Quotes: 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram', 'Reference models describe concepts of a domain... and have “a conformance relation that precisely identifies the set of conforming models”', 'SysML... ACME, AADL, MontiArc'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: No relevant evidence is present in the source documents. The texts focus on the technical implementation of digital shadows, friction modeling, and data traceability, without mentioning data ownership policies, privacy protection, or regulatory compliance.

No relevant evidence is present regarding cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, or authentication systems as defined in the characteristic description. While the text mentions a physical 'safety cell' ('The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range'), this describes a physical constraint of the asset rather than a digital security or fail-safe mechanism of the system.
